text: After completing adhesiolysis, the surgeon identifies the roux limb and the gastric remnant situated below it.
The surgeon frees the liver from the roux limb, allowing for retraction and exposure of the diaphragmatic hiatus.
The surgeon dissects the gastric pouch and roux limb away from the gastric remnant, carefully separating these structures to proceed with the esophagectomy and gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon carefully avoids the gastrocolic omentum during the dissection of the gastric pouch to prevent injury to the right gastroepiploic artery.
The video shows a clear view of the gastric remnant, gastric pouch, and roux limb, highlighting their spatial arrangement and anatomical relationships.
The surgeon applies anterior traction on the gastric pouch, carefully dividing its attachments up to the level of the left gastric artery after freeing the anticolic roux limb.
The surgeon exposes the right crus of the diaphragm and divides the phrenoesophageal ligament, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the esophageal hiatus.
The surgeon performs circumferential mobilization of the esophagus, advancing cephalad into the mediastinum, with the esophagus being carefully separated from surrounding mediastinal structures.
The surgeon incises the right cruce and central tendon to widen the hiatus, enhancing access to the esophageal area.
The surgeon divides the remaining adhesions on the left side of the gastric pouch, completing the mobilization of the esophagus, gastric pouch, and Roux limb.
The surgeon divides the jejunum just distal to the gastrojejunostomy, using surgical instruments to separate the intestinal segment.
The surgeon uses an endoscopic linear stapler to transect the jejunum, visibly separating the intestinal segment distal to the gastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon divides the mesenteric attachments to the jejunum, elevating the specimen to expose the left gastric artery.
The surgeon completes a celiac nodal dissection by skeletonizing the left gastric artery, reflecting the nodal tissue upwards towards the stomach, revealing the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to transect the left gastric artery, completing the dissection and separation of this vessel from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon places a Penrose drain around the esophagus and secures it in position, ensuring proper drainage and isolation of the esophageal structure.
The surgeon constructs the gastric conduit by mobilizing the greater curvature of the remnant stomach, ensuring preservation of the gastroepiploic pedicle.
The surgeon performs a pyloroplasty, visibly manipulating the pylorus to enhance gastric emptying by incising and suturing the pyloric muscle.
The surgeon begins creating the gastric conduit by further mobilizing the greater curvature of the remnant stomach, ensuring the gastroepiploic pedicle is preserved.
The surgeon shapes the gastric conduit by stapling along the lesser curvature of the stomach, using multiple firings of an endoscopic linear stapler to create the desired form.
The surgeon carefully shapes the gastric conduit, ensuring symmetry in its formation, as the conduit is constructed from the mobilized gastric remnant.
The surgeon sutures the distal end of the esophageal specimen to the end of the gastric conduit, preparing for its delivery into the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon places a sixteen French jejunostomy tube into the roux limb and secures it to the abdominal wall, ensuring proper positioning and fixation.
The surgeon divides the inferior pulmonary ligament and incises the pleura along the azygos vein, continuing until the vein arches over the esophagus.
The surgeon delivers a Penrose drain into the chest cavity to aid in the mobilization of the thoracic esophagus, ensuring the conduit remains symmetrical.
The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus circumferentially up to the level of the azygos vein, ensuring complete separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon mobilizes lymphatic tissues en bloc with the esophagus, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects and divides the azagus vein using a linear stapler with a curved tip, ensuring precise separation of the vein from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the proximal transection of the esophagus using a surgical stapler, visibly separating the esophagus from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon gently delivers the conduit into the chest cavity, followed by the removal of the specimen, which includes the esophagus, gastric pouch, and a few centimeters of jejunum.
The surgeon uses the Orville device to deliver the anvil into the proximal esophagus, preparing for the next step in the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon creates a gastrotomy in the gastric conduit to accommodate a 25mm EEA stapler, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a stapler anastomosis, connecting the proximal esophagus to the gastric conduit using a circular stapler.

The surgeon closes the gastrotomy in the gastric conduit using a stapler, and the completed esophagogastric anastomosis is visible, demonstrating the connection between the esophagus and gastric conduit.
